title: Patch a SQL Injection Vulnerability
tagline: Rewrite dangerous raw SQL queries using parameterized statements.
scenario: A legacy Node.js application searches for users by username. The query is written as: `db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + req.body.username + "'")`. A malicious user just dropped the users table using `' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE users; --`.
brief: Rewrite the vulnerable database interaction. You can either use parameterized queries with the raw driver (e.g., `pg` or `mysql2`), or implement an ORM (like Prisma or Sequelize) to handle escaping automatically.
deliverables: The patched code snippet showing the secure database query
An explanation of exactly how the malicious payload (`' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE users; --`) manipulated the original query string
submissionGuidance: The goal is to prove you understand parameterized queries. Simply writing a regex to block semicolons is not an acceptable solution.

rubric.label: Parameterized Query
rubric.description: Is the query correctly parameterized to prevent injection?

rubric.label: Attack Breakdown
rubric.description: Is the explanation of the attack payload accurate?

rubric.label: Best Practices
rubric.description: Does the candidate rely on standard drivers/ORMs rather than custom regex?
